---
title: "Backend-less widget in Observable JS (OJS)"
---

Every other tutorial that renders a widget with **no kernel** does it from Python
([save & restore](save-restore.qmd), this site's [home page](../index.qmd)) or a plain
browser page (`examples/web/index.html`). This page demonstrates a *third* angle on the
same claim — "the frontend is just JavaScript, run it anywhere" — using Quarto's
built-in **OJS (Observable JS) engine**, which executes JS *in the reader's browser* when
they view this page, with **no Python and no kernel** involved in rendering the widget
below.

> **Getting `@cositos/front` for your own project.** It is **not yet published to
> npm/jsr**. Until it is, vendor it: copy `front/src/*.js` from the repo into your project
> (they have zero external dependencies — only relative imports among themselves) and
> `import` from your copy. This docs site does exactly that (see below).

`@cositos/front` (the standalone `Model`/`Channel`/`loadWidget`/`renderWidget` runtime
behind `examples/web/index.html`, see [`docs/hosts.md`](hosts.md)) has zero external
dependencies — only relative imports between its own files — so this project's
`pre-render` step (see `docs/_quarto.yml`) copies `front/src/*.js` verbatim into
`docs/vendor/cositos-front/` before every build, and the OJS cells below `import()` it
from there. `docs/vendor/` is regenerated on every render (gitignored, like `_site/`), so
there is nothing to keep in sync by hand.

**Note the `../../`:** a dynamic `import()` inside a compiled OJS cell resolves relative
to Quarto's own runtime module (`site_libs/quarto-ojs/…`), not to this page's URL — two
levels up lands back at the site root regardless of how deeply this page is nested, so
`../../vendor/...` is correct here (and everywhere else an OJS cell needs a site asset).

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
front = await import("../../vendor/cositos-front/index.js")
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
counterEsm = `
export default {
  render({ model, el }) {
    const btn = document.createElement("button");
    const out = document.createElement("span");
    const paint = () => { out.textContent = " count = " + model.get("n"); };
    btn.textContent = "increment";
    btn.addEventListener("click", () => {
      model.set("n", model.get("n") + 1);
      model.save_changes();
    });
    model.on("change:n", paint);
    paint();
    el.appendChild(btn);
    el.appendChild(out);
  }
}
`
```

```{ojs}
counter = {
  const { Model, LocalChannel, loadWidget, renderWidget } = front;
  const el = DOM.element("div", {
    style: "border:1px solid #ddd;border-radius:8px;padding:1rem;max-width:16rem;",
  });
  const model = new Model({ n: 0 }, new LocalChannel());
  const widget = await loadWidget(counterEsm);
  await renderWidget(widget, { model, el });
  return el;
}
```

Clicking **increment** above runs entirely client-side: `LocalChannel` is a loopback
channel with no backend (see [`docs/hosts.md`](hosts.md#localchannel--client-only-widgets)),
so `model.save_changes()` updates in-browser state and nothing else. The button's ESM
(the `counterEsm` string above) is the *same* shape of module a Jupyter-kernel widget
would ship — `export default { render({ model, el }) { ... } }` — nothing OJS-specific
about it; only the `Channel` differs per host (see [status](status.md) for the full
per-host matrix).

## Next steps

- [Author a widget](authoring-widgets.qmd) — write your own widget's ESM.
- [Widget gallery](widgets-gallery.qmd) — more browser-rendered widgets.
- [Static export](static-export.qmd) — the Python route to the same kernel-free output.
